Leila de Lima’s story is one of courage, resilience, and quiet strength — a stark contrast to Rodrigo Duterte, whose power relied on fear and violence. This reflection explores her nearly seven-year ordeal — from unjust imprisonment to her unwavering defiance — and how she found purpose after surviving it all.

Some mornings, as I sit with my cup of coffee, I wonder how much a person can endure before they break. Leila de Lima’s story makes me think about that. She spent nearly seven years behind bars — unjustly accused, locked away from her family, and denied the chance to say goodbye to loved ones. They called her a criminal, but the evidence was weak, the testimonies shaky, and the motive clear. She had spoken out against a brutal war on drugs, and for that, they tried to silence her.

Inside her cramped cell, she lived with the constant threat of violence. She was once held hostage at knifepoint — a blade pressed against her chest by a desperate man who had nothing to lose. She could have died that day, and no one would have been surprised. Yet somehow, she refused to crumble. Instead, she found a way to hold on — to her thoughts, to her faith, to the quiet belief that the truth would win out in the end. I imagine her holding on to that belief like someone gripping a hot cup of coffee on a cold morning — drawing warmth from it, steadying herself, willing herself not to let go.

The Ordeal Begins: Arrest and Isolation
The moment they took her away, Leila de Lima knew what it meant. I think about what it must have been like — that moment when they took her away. Leila de Lima had always known the risks. Speaking out against Duterte’s war on drugs was dangerous — the kind of dangerous that doesn’t just threaten your career but your life itself. Still, she spoke.

And when they came for her, it was as if the world had been waiting for it. Cameras swarmed to capture the moment she was led away, the woman they called a criminal. Never mind that the accusations were weak, built on shaky testimonies from inmates desperate to save themselves ( [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/6/24/philippine-court-clears-duterte-critic-leila-de-lima-of-drugs-charges) ).

Inside her cell at Camp Crame, the noise stopped. No cameras, no voices — just silence and the cold reality of four walls. Her family couldn’t visit. Her friends were kept away. Even her mother — 91 years old — was left believing that de Lima was on a study leave in the United States. It wasn’t just a lie. It was protection — a way to spare her the knowledge that her daughter was locked away.

In those first days, de Lima barely slept. Anger kept her restless — the kind of anger that leaves you exhausted but unable to focus on anything except the injustice of it all. But anger wasn’t enough. She needed something stronger — something to keep her from falling apart. So she gave herself a rule: “Don’t Trust, Don’t Fear, Don’t Beg.” It became her armor — a whisper in her mind that reminded her to stay still when her thoughts raced and when fear tried to creep in. In a place designed to break her, she chose to hold on. And she refused to let go.

Survival in Detention: Lessons in Endurance
Some people say you get used to anything if you live with it long enough. But I wonder if you ever really get used to being alone — not just physically, but in a way that leaves you wondering if the world has forgotten you. That’s what Leila de Lima faced, day after day, for nearly seven years.

She learned quickly that time inside a cell doesn’t pass the way it does outside. There’s no rhythm of traffic or the quiet calm of evening — just hours stacked on top of each other, too quiet to ignore. So she built her own rhythm. Mornings were for prayer. Afternoons were for reading. Evenings were for writing — letters filled with the words she had kept bottled up. Each day was carefully planned, not because she wanted structure, but because she needed it to survive. Without it, she knew her mind would drift — and that was dangerous.

She refused to eat the food they served. It wasn’t paranoia — it was survival. After all, what’s to stop someone from slipping something into your meal when you’re a prisoner with powerful enemies? ( [Amnesty International](https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/news/2023/02/philippines-leila-de-lima-detention/) )

But even with routine, even with purpose, there were moments when no amount of mental discipline could block out the pain of it all. When PNoy died, she wasn’t allowed to attend his wake. When her close friends Dinky Soliman and Mon Jimenez passed away, she was kept away again ( [GMA News](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/opinion/content/898567/when-leila-de-lima-was-taken-hostage-in-prison/story/) ). Not being there — not being able to say goodbye — crushed her.

Her 91-year-old mother never knew about any of it. Her family kept her mother believing that de Lima was still on her study leave in the United States. That lie — painful as it was — spared her mother from heartbreak. But for de Lima, it was another kind of grief. There’s something cruel about losing people while you’re locked away — knowing the world is moving forward without you, and all you can do is sit with the ache.

Still, she refused to fall apart. Instead, she wrote. Letters. Essays. Pages filled with thoughts that needed somewhere to go. Writing became more than just a way to pass the time — it was an act of defiance. Each word was proof that her mind still belonged to her. They could keep her body locked away. But her voice? That was hers. And she refused to let it die.

A Brush with Death: The Hostage Crisis
There’s something terrifying about danger when you can’t see it coming. For years, Leila de Lima had adjusted to the rhythm of prison life — the silence, the isolation, the feeling of being hidden and forgotten. But on October 9, 2022, that silence was shattered.

That morning, an inmate named Feliciano Sulayao — a man linked to the Abu Sayyaf Group — forced his way into her cell. He was holding a knife. He grabbed her, pressed the blade against her chest, and pulled her close. One move — one twitch of his wrist — and it would have been over.

“I honestly thought that was it,” she later admitted, recalling the moment she felt the cold edge of the knife against her ( [BenarNews](https://www.benarnews.org/english/news/philippine/de-lima-hostage-prison-10092022070100.html) ). Sulayao had nothing to lose. He had already killed another inmate moments earlier. Now, holding de Lima, he was making a desperate attempt to escape — and she knew it.

Still, she stayed calm. In that terrifying moment, when fear could have taken over, de Lima kept her composure. She didn’t scream. She held her breath and waited. Moments later, her captor was shot dead by responding officers. It was over. But the fear lingered.

De Lima later described it as a “near-death experience” — one that left her body aching and her mind racing long after the knife was gone ( [Amnesty International](https://www.amnesty.org.ph/2022/10/immediate-release-not-transfer-of-detention-for-leila-de-lima-after-hostage-taking-incident/) ). But what made it worse was what came after. Authorities didn’t investigate how Sulayao had managed to reach her cell — no inquiry, no accountability. Instead, they offered to transfer her to another facility. As if moving her would somehow erase the fact that they had failed to protect her.

For de Lima, it was yet another reminder that she wasn’t just forgotten — she was vulnerable. And even after everything she had endured, her life still wasn’t hers to control.

Strength Through Advocacy: Standing Firm Against Injustice
They wanted her silent. But Leila de Lima refused to stay quiet. Even in detention — cut off from the world, denied her freedom, and surrounded by walls meant to break her — she remained a loud voice for justice. From her cell, she wrote letters — sharp, unflinching words that called out Duterte’s war on drugs and the system that allowed it. She condemned the extrajudicial killings that had claimed thousands of Filipino lives. She warned that silence, while tempting, only strengthens those who abuse power.

They thought they could erase her. Instead, her voice traveled farther than ever before. Her words reached supporters, journalists, and human rights organizations. Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International, and the European Parliament all called for her release, denouncing her imprisonment as politically motivated and unjust ( [Human Rights Watch](https://www.hrw.org/news/2023/06/08/outspoken-philippine-ex-senator-denied-bail) , [Amnesty International](https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/campaigns/2024/03/leila-de-lima-philippines/) ).

Even in Washington, her name was spoken. Five U.S. Senators, including Marco Rubio, Edward Markey, and Richard Durbin, filed a bipartisan resolution urging her release — a rare move that showed her case had become more than just a local issue ( [Philippine Senate](https://legacy.senate.gov.ph/press_release/2022/0227_delima2.asp) ).

From her tiny cell in Camp Crame, de Lima became a symbol — not just of resistance, but of how truth can penetrate even the thickest walls. When asked if she could forgive Duterte, her answer revealed how much the years had taken from her: “Not yet… That’s why I said, God forgive him… He has practically ruined my life. It’s immeasurable.” ( [Time](https://time.com/6339253/philippines-leila-de-lima-interview-duterte-justice/) )

But forgiveness wasn’t her focus — accountability was. Even after her release, she vowed to assist the International Criminal Court (ICC) in gathering evidence against Duterte’s bloody war on drugs ( [GMA News](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/topstories/nation/911212/leila-de-lima-eyeing-case-vs-duterte-others-over-detention/story/) ).

Nearly seven years in detention — and still, she refused to let her voice be buried. They locked her away, but her words kept finding their way out. And people kept listening.

Finding Freedom: Vindication After 2,454 Days
Freedom shouldn’t feel unfamiliar. But after nearly seven years behind bars, stepping outside wasn’t as simple as walking through an open door. On June 24, 2024, a Philippine court finally cleared Leila de Lima of the last remaining charges against her. The accusations — built on coerced testimonies and lies — had collapsed under their own weight. The judge’s words were clear: the prosecution had failed to prove her guilt ( [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/6/24/philippine-court-clears-duterte-critic-leila-de-lima-of-drugs-charges) ).

It should have been a moment of celebration. But for de Lima, it felt heavier than that. “I am now completely free and vindicated. It’s very liberating,” she told reporters outside the courtroom. Yet there was no joy in her voice — only quiet relief. It’s hard to celebrate when you know the truth all along.

She had spent 2,454 days in detention — nearly seven years away from her family, her career, and the life she had worked so hard to build. Those were days she could never get back. When her mother — now in her nineties — finally learned the truth, it wasn’t in the way de Lima had hoped. Her family had kept her in the dark to spare her the pain, telling her daughter was studying abroad. The reunion that should have been filled with relief instead felt like a reminder of how much time had been lost.

De Lima went back to teaching law — a quiet return to what she once knew. There was no dramatic speech, no loud declaration of victory. Instead, she chose to focus on what remained: her family, her faith, and whatever pieces of her old life she could still hold on to. And somehow, after everything, she kept going. Not because she forgot what happened. But because she refused to let it define the rest of her life.

A Legacy of Courage: Turning Loss into Purpose
Freedom wasn’t the end of Leila de Lima’s story. When the cell door finally opened, she didn’t just step back into her old life — because that life was gone. Nearly seven years had been taken from her. Seven years where friends passed away, milestones came and went, and her mother — now frail and aging — had spent her final years believing her daughter was somewhere else.

That kind of loss changes you. But de Lima didn’t return as someone broken by those years. Instead, she returned as someone determined to make those years mean something. In October 2024, standing before graduates at De La Salle-College of Saint Benilde, she shared the wisdom she had carried through her darkest days ( [Benilde](https://www.benilde.edu.ph/ten-life-lessons-atty-leila-de-lima-shared-with-benildean-graduates/) ). Her words weren’t about endurance — they were about living with purpose after the storm has passed.

“Life isn’t just about surviving,” she told them. “It’s about finding your reason to keep moving forward.” She spoke of how grief — if left unchecked — can become a prison of its own. How anger, no matter how justified, can eventually consume the very person who carries it.

“I refused to let bitterness take hold,” she said. “Because when you let bitterness win, they take more from you than your freedom — they take your future too.”

Instead of dwelling on what she lost, de Lima chose to focus on what remained. She returned to teaching law — a quiet step toward normalcy. She spent time with her family, piecing back together the moments that had slipped away. And she spoke — not just about her suffering, but about what comes after suffering. About how life can be shattered, but how it can still move forward.

De Lima’s story isn’t just about defiance or survival. It’s about what you do once you’re free again. And somehow, despite everything, she found a way to live.

Conclusion: Finding Strength in a Cup of Coffee
Some mornings, as I sit with my coffee, I think about Leila de Lima. I think about what it means to hold on — not just in the loud moments when courage demands to be seen, but in the quiet, empty hours when no one’s watching.

For nearly seven years, she endured the kind of suffering that breaks most people. They tried to silence her, to isolate her, to make her forget who she was. But somehow, she held on — not through rage or bitterness, but through something quieter: Routine. Discipline. Faith.

I imagine her sitting alone in her cell, morning after morning, whispering the words she clung to: “Don’t Trust. Don’t Fear. Don’t Beg.” Not because she believed those words could change her situation — but because they kept her steady when the world felt like it was slipping away.

That’s the kind of strength that doesn’t always make headlines — the strength to survive one day, and then the one after that. When she was finally set free, there was no victory speech. No fist in the air. Just a woman who had found herself in silence, and walked out determined to live again.

Some mornings, when I take that first sip of coffee, I wonder if strength really is like that — not a roar, but a quiet decision to keep going, no matter how impossible it feels. And somehow, despite everything, she kept going. And maybe that’s what strength really looks like.

Vico Sotto has proven himself a capable and principled leader, but conversations about him becoming the next VP or president may do more harm than good. This blog explores why excessive pressure can hurt reformers like Vico — and outlines five meaningful ways we can protect him without turning admiration into blind loyalty.

Sunday morning. I'm at the tennis court again, waiting for my daughter as she trains. I decided to have my morning coffee and start writing while I wait. The air is cool, and the quiet rhythm of the court fills the space — the shuffle of shoes, the soft thud of tennis balls, the occasional voice of her coach giving instructions. I take a sip of coffee and watch my daughter move across the court. She’s focused, steady, and determined — not rushing, just doing the work. Her training mates are the same, each locked in their own drills. There’s no noise, no fanfare — just quiet dedication.

It makes me think of Vico Sotto. His name has been everywhere lately. On Threads, people are calling him the perfect running mate for Leni Robredo — some even say he should run for president. I agree with them. Vico is sharp, principled, and committed to real change. But talking about him this way — as if he’s destined for Malacañang — makes me uneasy. Leaders like him don’t come along often, and putting him on a pedestal this early doesn’t just risk derailing his work. It paints a target on his back.

### The Rise of Vico Sotto: A Leader Who Chose Substance Over Spectacle

Vico Sotto’s rise in politics felt different. While most campaigns relied on familiar tactics — grand rallies, catchy jingles, and celebrity endorsements — Vico’s approach was strikingly different. In 2019, he ran for mayor of Pasig City, challenging a political dynasty that had ruled for 27 years. The odds were stacked against him, yet he refused to play the game the traditional way. There were no comedy routines or song-and-dance numbers in his campaign. Despite being the son of Vic Sotto and Coney Reyes, he didn't rely on his celebrity background.

“As politicians, we shouldn't underestimate the Filipino voter. I refused to make it a show. It was about governance,” he once said in an interview. That message was clear — governance built on transparency, accountability, and meaningful change. His victory wasn’t just a win for Pasig. It was a statement — proof that voters were willing to embrace leaders who treat them with respect.

As mayor, he stayed true to his platform. Instead of headline-grabbing stunts, he focused on practical reforms. During his campaign, Vico presented a Five-Point Platform — a clear roadmap for reform:
1. Healthcare as an Anti-Poverty Measure: He prioritized accessible healthcare services and ensured medical supplies were properly funded.
2. Housing Programs: He promised informal settlers they wouldn’t be relocated without proper services and livelihood support.
3. Accessible Education: He simplified scholarship applications, removing unnecessary requirements that often discouraged students.
4. Public Consultation: He involved citizens in decision-making to promote transparency in government projects.
5. Anti-Corruption Efforts: He created a Government Efficiency and Anti-Corruption Commission and launched a hotline where residents could report corrupt practices.

These weren’t just promises — they were practical steps designed to fix problems that had plagued Pasig for decades. In a country where political campaigns are often fueled by showmanship and empty slogans, Vico’s quiet, work-first approach was a breath of fresh air.

### Leadership Qualities That Define Him

Good leaders talk about values. Great leaders live by them. Vico Sotto belongs to the latter. His leadership isn’t defined by slogans or speeches — it’s shaped by his actions.

#### Integrity
This is what sets him apart the most. When a city hall employee — someone Vico personally recommended — was caught soliciting a bribe, he didn’t hesitate to act. He didn’t protect his friend; he protected the integrity of his office. “Kaibigan ko [siya], ako po nagrecommend sa kanya na ipasok siya sa city hall, pero napatunayan natin na gumawa siya ng mali,” he said. It was a difficult decision, but Vico held firm. For him, there are no exceptions when it comes to corruption.

#### Empathy
While other local leaders poured resources into flashy infrastructure projects, Vico redirected funds toward social services. He knew his city didn’t need grand statements — it needed real help. That meant ensuring hospitals were stocked, social workers were supported, and low-income families had access to aid.

#### Visionary Thinking
In Pasig City, accessing public services used to mean relying on connections. Vico tackled this by shifting to data-driven governance. Decisions on resource distribution, assistance programs, and infrastructure were based on actual needs, not political favors. His vision was simple: services should reach those who need them most, not just those who know someone at city hall.

#### Action-Oriented Leadership
When COVID-19 hit, Vico’s administration didn’t wait for national guidelines — they acted. Health workers were briefed early, barangays received disinfectant kits, and sanitation facilities were established. His proactive approach allowed Pasig to respond swiftly, protecting residents when uncertainty was at its highest.

#### Humility and Authenticity
Despite his accomplishments, Vico avoids the spotlight. He rejects the “hero” label, crediting his team for Pasig’s progress. His direct, no-frills communication style resonates because people can tell he isn’t reading from a script. His sincerity isn’t staged — it’s real.

### Lessons from Jesse and Leni Robredo: A Warning for Reformers

Admiring good leaders is natural. But in Philippine politics, admiration can sometimes be dangerous.

#### Jesse Robredo’s Story
As mayor of Naga City, Jesse introduced reforms that transformed local governance. He pushed for transparency and people empowerment. But even Jesse’s honesty didn’t shield him from attacks. Despite his clean record, he became the target of smears that questioned his credentials. He remained focused on his work, but the attacks revealed a hard truth: even the most honest leaders aren’t safe from misinformation. Just as his influence was growing on a national scale, his life was cut short in a plane crash.

#### Leni Robredo’s Experience
Following her husband's death, Leni Robredo served as Vice President. During her tenure, she launched the Angat Buhay program. Despite limited resources, her office received the ISO 9001:2015 certification for its quality management systems. However, her tenure was marred by relentless disinformation campaigns. Fabricated stories about her flooded social media. By the time her team realized the scale of these attacks, it was too late. “Correcting [and] fact checking are only heard in our echo chambers because there is already a separate reality,” Leni later admitted.

#### The Connection to Vico Sotto
These stories remind me of what’s happening with Vico Sotto. He’s already faced false claims, including a doctored quote attacking former President Rodrigo Duterte — a statement Vico never made. It’s a pattern we’ve seen before. The more people praise a leader like Vico, the more vulnerable they become to these kinds of attacks. This isn’t to say Vico shouldn’t be admired — he deserves respect. But turning him into a symbol of national change too early risks putting a target on his back. If we want Vico to succeed, we need to protect him from that.

### How to Truly Protect Vico Sotto: Beyond Just Saying 'Protect Vico'

Saying “Protect Vico” isn’t enough. Real protection demands action.
1. Monitor the News — and Be Vigilant About What’s Being Said: Smear campaigns often start small. Stay informed and follow credible news sources.
2. Expose Damaging Attacks Early: Lies thrive when they’re ignored. If someone spreads false claims about Vico, expose them early.
3. Fact-Check and Disprove Misinformation: When a fabricated quote or misleading story appears, respond with facts and link credible sources.
4. Support His Programs — Not Just His Image: Vico’s strength lies in his work. Share updates about his projects, like the Peso Smart Financial Literacy Program.
5. Be Willing to Show Up — Physically, If Needed: Protection means more than defending him online. If powerful forces try to sabotage his work, the public must be ready to stand by him.

### Conclusion: Let Him Lead, Let Him Finish What He Started

Vico Sotto deserves admiration. His leadership has shown that change is possible. But admiration shouldn’t turn into pressure. Conversations about him becoming vice president — or even president — are premature. They don’t just distract from the work he’s doing in Pasig; they make him a bigger target for those who want reform to fail. If we truly believe Vico represents hope, the best way to support him is to give him space to lead. Let him build his record. Let him strengthen the foundation of good governance in Pasig. Protecting a leader isn’t about blind loyalty. It’s about honest support — defending their good work while respecting their own timing. If Vico decides to seek higher office one day, that should be his call — not ours. Until then, let him lead. Let him finish what he started.

Harry Roque spent years defending Duterte’s bloody war on drugs — now he’s seeking asylum in the Netherlands. But asylum is for victims, not those who justify violence. Here's why the Netherlands should say no.

News broke recently that Harry Roque, Duterte’s former spokesperson, may be seeking asylum in the Netherlands.
I had to read that twice.
Roque, once a respected human rights lawyer, stood with grieving families and fought for justice. But somewhere along the way, he switched sides. He went from defending victims to defending the very administration accused of robbing those same families of their loved ones.
He became Duterte’s mouthpiece — the man who stood behind podiums insisting that no one was killed lawlessly, even as thousands of bodies piled up in the streets.
Thousands.

The Pain Roque Chose to Ignore
Official records say over 6,000 people were killed in Duterte’s drug war. Human rights groups believe the real number could be closer to 30,000. Either way, that’s thousands of lives cut short — most without trial, without investigation, and without justice.
And these weren’t drug lords or cartel bosses. Some were tricycle drivers. Some were teenagers. Some were fathers who never made it home.
While families grieved, Roque stood at the podium, claiming that the justice system was working — as if those mourning mothers weren’t burying their sons because of that very system’s failure.
I wonder how they felt watching him speak.

Roque’s Role in Blocking Accountability
When the International Criminal Court (ICC) started investigating the drug war, Duterte’s administration fought back by pulling the Philippines out of the ICC altogether — a move widely seen as an attempt to dodge accountability.
Roque didn’t make that decision. But he defended it every step of the way.
He called the ICC politically motivated. He claimed they had no right to investigate. He argued that the Philippine justice system was functioning just fine — never mind the families who knew better.
And now?
Now he’s asking for asylum in the Netherlands — the very country that hosts the ICC.
That’s not just ironic. That’s offensive.

Unanswered Questions About POGO
Roque’s asylum bid also comes at a suspicious time.
Recent Senate investigations in the Philippines have linked him to the Philippine Offshore Gaming Operators (POGO) — an industry tangled in corruption, trafficking, and organized crime. While nothing has been proven yet, Roque’s involvement raises questions.
Questions he hasn’t answered.
Now, instead of facing those questions, he’s trying to slip away.

The Netherlands Should Say No
Asylum is for the persecuted — not for those running from accountability.
The Netherlands has a reputation for standing firm on justice. This is a country that hosts the ICC — a nation that believes the powerful shouldn’t escape consequences.
Letting Roque stay would send the wrong message. It would tell the world that those who defend violence and enable oppression can still find a safe place to hide.
The families mourning their loved ones deserve better than that.
They deserve answers. They deserve accountability.
And they deserve to know that the man who spent years excusing their suffering won’t be granted the same protection they were denied.
Justice demands accountability. The Netherlands shouldn’t give Roque a free pass — not when so many are still waiting for the justice he tried so hard to deny them.

Fake news thrives in noise — loud claims wrapped in vague words like "daw" or disguised as "my truth." In this blog, I reflect on the recent congressional hearing where vloggers apologized for spreading false information, the role of social media algorithms, and why accountability doesn't stop with content creators. Sometimes, an apology isn't about regret — it's about survival. And sometimes, the truth is quiet, steady, and easy to miss.

I was sitting on a worn-out bench at the tennis court, sipping a 3-in-1 Nescafé while waiting for my turn to play. The court’s dull thuds from rackets hitting balls filled the air, while scattered conversations from nearby tables hummed in the background. But my mind was elsewhere.

The recent House Tri-Committee hearing on fake news has been stuck in my head. Vloggers breaking down in tears, politicians firing tough questions, and the word "allegedly" being used like a shield. It reminded me of something I've often seen — not just online, but in casual conversations among friends, neighbors, and family.

“Sabi daw…”
“May nagsabi…”
“Allegedly…”

It’s that familiar dance where people avoid owning their words, clinging to vague qualifiers to escape accountability. In the Philippines, we have a word for it: daw. It’s a tiny word with a massive impact. It turns rumors into news and makes gossip feel like truth — and that’s where the danger lies.

As I sipped my coffee, I wondered: How often have we excused false claims by convincing ourselves it’s just “someone’s truth”? Maybe more often than we’d like to admit.

**When 'My Truth' Becomes Fake News**
MJ Quiambao Reyes sat before the House Tri-Committee, defending her claim that extrajudicial killings (EJKs) under the Duterte administration were a “massive hoax.” Her explanation? “It’s my truth.”

That phrase bothered me. There’s a difference between standing by a personal opinion and presenting something as fact. Yet Reyes used “my truth” as a way to validate a claim that lacked any evidence. I’ve seen this happen outside politics, too. Maybe you’ve heard someone insist that a relative’s health scare was caused by a rumored vaccine side effect because they “heard it from a friend.”

It’s easy to fall into that trap. Sometimes, we’re so convinced by what we feel is true that we forget to ask if it actually is. That's what made Reyes' defense unsettling. When lawmakers pressed her for evidence, she had none. She admitted she couldn’t prove her post. Yet she held onto “her truth” as if it were enough.

That’s the tricky thing about justifying misinformation. People cling to their version of the truth because letting go feels like losing an argument — or worse, admitting they were wrong.

**The Power of 'Daw' as a Shield**
Krizette Chu knew her post was questionable. She claimed there would be mass resignations in the police and military after Duterte’s arrest — a story that could have triggered national instability. When lawmakers asked her to explain, she didn’t deny it. Instead, she said she added the word daw.

That one word was her defense. It’s a familiar tactic. I’ve heard people do this countless times — casually sharing rumors, then slipping in a “sabi daw” just in case they’re wrong. But here’s the thing: adding daw doesn’t erase the damage.

People still believed Chu’s claim. Some may have shared it as fact, while others felt uneasy about what might happen next. Even with a daw attached, the story had already left its mark. That’s the danger of daw. For Filipinos, the word disappears as a story spreads.

“Mars, bakla daw si Mark, sabi ni Jennifer.”
At first, there’s hesitation — a hint that it’s just a rumor. But pass that story to a few more people, and the daw quietly vanishes.
“Alam mo bang bakla si Mark?”

The same happens when the story starts as a question.
“Mars, bakla ba si Mike?”
Later, it becomes:
“Alam mo ba sabi sakin ni Jennifer, bakla si Mike.”

From interrogative to declarative. From rumor to fact real quick. And before long, poor Mark is just another victim of a story that spiraled out of control — the truth buried under layers of “daw.” Chu’s defense may have sounded clever in the hearing, but it’s the same excuse I’ve heard at dining tables and in group chats. It’s never harmless.

**The Justification of Misinformation**
Some content creators know exactly what they’re doing. During the hearing, both Krizette Chu and MJ Quiambao Reyes defended their posts by claiming they were “raising awareness” or “starting a conversation.” It’s a clever excuse — one that makes misinformation sound noble.

I've seen this pattern before. Someone spreads a rumor, then masks it as concern.
“Alam mo, hindi ako sigurado pero... narinig ko lang... nag-aalala lang ako para sa kanila.”

It sounds harmless at first — even thoughtful. But more often than not, that “concern” is just a cover for spreading something they can’t prove. Vloggers like Chu and Reyes played a similar game. Their posts weren’t framed as solid facts, yet they were designed to plant seeds of doubt.

“I was just asking questions” or “I just wanted to raise awareness.”
But here’s the thing — planting doubt is just as damaging as spreading lies. A neighbor whispering “Hindi naman ako sigurado, pero baka nga may babae si Mang Tonyo” doesn’t need proof. The damage is done. Even if the rumor started with hesitation, doubt lingers — and that doubt is what people remember.

It’s the same online. A vlogger doesn’t need to prove their claim when they can frame it as “just something people should think about.” That’s how misinformation keeps finding a way to slip through — dressed up as concern, disguised as a conversation starter.

**Echo Chambers and Social Media’s Role in Fake News**
All three vloggers — Krizette Chu, MJ Quiambao Reyes, and Mark Lopez — admitted something troubling during the hearing. Their posts weren’t based on verified facts. They relied on “what people were saying online.” In other words, they trusted the noise inside their own echo chambers.

That’s the problem with social media — it’s designed to make us feel right. Algorithms feed users content that matches what they already believe. And the more you hear something, the more believable it sounds — even when it's wrong.

I’ve seen this happen firsthand on Threads. Lately, I’ve noticed many fellow Kakampinks calling Threads our “new home,” convinced that the platform is now dominated by our side. I was happy to see it. It felt comforting — like we had carved out a space where our voices mattered most.

But here’s the problem. It’s not that Threads belongs to us — it’s that the algorithm is feeding us what it thinks we want. Out of curiosity, I searched the hashtags used by the DDS. Turns out, they were saying the exact same thing. They were celebrating Threads as *their* new platform because their feeds were just as packed with posts from their side.

Both groups felt like they had taken over, yet neither had seen what was happening on the other side of the digital wall. That’s how social media tricks us. Algorithms create bubbles that make us feel like our ideas dominate the world. It’s comforting — but dangerously misleading.

This false sense of dominance fuels misinformation. When everyone around you is saying the same thing, there’s less reason to question it — and more reason to assume it’s true. That’s why content creators like Chu, Reyes, and Lopez believed their own posts so strongly. They were trapped in a system that kept feeding those falsehoods back to them.

**Performative Apologies and Emotional Manipulation**
“Sige po, mag-apologize po ako. Sige po, fake news po ako.”
Mark Lopez’s words may have sounded like an admission, but listen closely — it didn’t sound like regret. There’s something about the way he said it that feels off.

“Sige po...” — a phrase Filipinos often use when they feel cornered. It’s the language of someone who’s tired of arguing, giving in just to get it over with.
“Mag-apologize po ako...” — polite, yet distant. It felt more like he was checking a box than genuinely taking responsibility.
And then, “Fake news po ako.”

The words felt like surrender — not accountability. Almost as if he was saying: “Fine, if you want me to say it, I’ll say it.” That’s the tricky part about these congressional hearings. Sometimes, apologizing isn’t really a choice. In front of someone like Rep. Benny Abante — whose presence alone can be intimidating — refusing to back down can have serious consequences. For someone like Lopez, a forced apology may have felt safer than pushing back. It’s less about regret and more about survival.

It reminded me of how kids sometimes apologize when they know they’re about to get punished.
“Sorry na po...”
Not because they understand what they did wrong — but because they’re hoping to avoid what comes next. You say sorry para hindi ka mapalo ng nanay mo. (I wish this worked for my adoptive mother.)

That’s what Lopez’s words felt like. His tears — and those of Krizette Chu and MJ Quiambao Reyes — may have been real. Maybe the pressure of the hearing was too much. But part of me wonders if they also knew something else — that sometimes, crying makes people stop asking questions.

**Accountability Beyond Content Creators**
The problem with fake news in the Philippines goes beyond individual vloggers. For years, figures like Mocha Uson and Drew Olivar have played a significant role in shaping this disinformation landscape. They didn’t just spread false claims — they worked to convince Filipinos that mainstream media couldn’t be trusted.

News outlets were labeled biased and corrupt, while Uson, Olivar, and their followers painted themselves as the only source of real information. And for some, it worked. People who once relied on traditional media began turning to these self-proclaimed truth-tellers — and that’s when the line between facts and fiction started to blur.

This distrust gave social media influencers an alarming kind of influence. Facts were easily dismissed, while rumors were embraced as hidden truths. That’s why the House Tri-Committee didn’t just confront the vloggers — they also called out the platforms that allow misinformation to thrive. Facebook and TikTok were issued show cause orders for ignoring previous hearings, signaling that lawmakers are finally looking for accountability at the source.

And they should. While content creators are responsible for what they post, social media platforms decide what gets seen. Algorithms reward engagement — not accuracy — so the most outrageous claims often rise to the top, while the truth gets buried. The worst part? Platforms usually act after the damage is done.

By the time a false claim is flagged, it’s already been shared thousands of times. Correcting it becomes nearly impossible. We’ve seen this during elections and heated political moments. Outrageous stories spread like wildfire, while the actual facts struggle to find an audience.

This is why accountability can't stop with content creators. Social media giants — the ones designing the algorithms and profiting from the noise — have to do better. Because when influencers like Uson, Olivar, Chu, Reyes, and Lopez succeed in convincing people that only they hold the truth, social media feeds become battlegrounds where falsehoods thrive — and real information gets lost in the noise.

**The Legal Consequences of Fake News**
Spreading fake news isn’t just careless — it can get you into serious trouble. In the Philippines, Republic Act 11469, also known as the Bayanihan to Heal as One Act, makes it a criminal offense to create or spread false information during emergencies. The law was introduced at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, when panic-driven rumors flooded social media.

And it wasn’t harmless. False claims caused panic buying, hoarding, and unnecessary fear. Some people skipped critical medical treatments because of a "sabi daw" they saw online. That’s the danger of fake news — even a single post can trigger real-world chaos.

The law makes it clear: disinformation isn’t just unethical — it’s punishable. But holding people accountable isn’t always simple. The challenge lies in proving intent. Vloggers like Krizette Chu, MJ Quiambao Reyes, and Mark Lopez knew this. They used qualifiers like "allegedly," claimed they were “just sharing what they heard,” or insisted they were “just asking questions.”

And that’s where it gets tricky. These defenses — vague enough to avoid direct blame — make it difficult to prove that someone intended to mislead others. But that doesn’t mean they’re innocent. Whether the intent was deliberate or careless, the damage is the same. False claims — especially in moments of crisis — fuel confusion, endanger lives, and undermine trust in our institutions.

Fake news isn’t just reckless noise online. It’s a risk that can follow you offline — to a courtroom, a jail cell, or even the end of a career.

**Final Sip of Thought**
My cup of 3-in-1 Nescafé had long gone cold. The thuds of tennis balls hitting rackets felt distant now — my mind still stuck on everything I had seen during the hearing.

Fake news thrives because it’s loud. It taps into what people want to believe and drowns out the quieter voice of truth. That’s why it’s so effective — and so dangerous. Vloggers like Krizette Chu, MJ Quiambao Reyes, and Mark Lopez played a part in spreading false claims, but they were also part of a larger system that prioritizes noise over real truth.

But accountability doesn’t end with them. Social media platforms, with their engagement-hungry algorithms, are part of the problem. And so are the words we use. I’ve seen it happen too often — posts filled with "daw," vague claims, or sensational headlines spreading faster than facts can catch up.

The damage isn’t always obvious. Sometimes, it’s a neighbor quietly worrying about a false crisis. Other times, it’s a family torn apart by rumors that spiraled out of control. That’s why we can’t just shrug it off as "just another post." There’s always something — or someone — at stake.

As I stared at my empty cup, I realized this — truth is rarely loud. It doesn’t demand attention the way fake news does. But if you pause long enough, it’s always there — quiet, steady, and waiting to be heard.

Fact-Checking Without Fact-Checkers: How Filipinos Can Verify Information Before Sharing

Discover essential fact-checking strategies to identify misinformation, counter DDS trolls, and avoid falling victim to online manipulation, deception, and propaganda.

I was halfway through my morning coffee when a memory hit me — a moment from years ago that still stings. 

As a freelance content writer, fact-checking has become second nature. But there was a time when I slipped. I once ghostwrote an article based on a source that looked credible but turned out to be biased. I didn’t realize it until after the piece was published. 

That mistake didn’t just change how I write — it changed how I consume information, especially on social media. 

Now, I pause before hitting ‘Share.’ I ask myself: 

Is this true? Where did this come from? What’s the motive behind this post? Who shared it? And more importantly, does it actually help anyone? 

These questions have become part of my routine because misinformation rarely comes from nowhere — there’s almost always a reason behind it. 

But what puzzles me is how many Filipinos — intelligent, thoughtful people — skip this step altogether. Some simply trust what they see online, while others want to fact-check but don’t know how. 

That’s why this article exists. 

If you’ve ever wondered how to verify information on your own — without relying on fact-checking organizations — this guide is for you.

## Why Fact-Checking Matters in the Philippines

The Philippines has been called “Patient Zero” in the global information disorder, having experienced large-scale political disinformation campaigns long before Brexit and the 2016 U.S. elections ([Fulcrum](https://fulcrum.sg/counter-disinformation-beyond-fact-checking-insights-from-the-philippines/)). 

Over the years, disinformation tactics have become more sophisticated. Fake social media personas, Instagram “thirst trap” influencers, and coordinated TikTok trends are now common strategies designed to manipulate public perception ([Media Support](https://www.mediasupport.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Countering-disinformation-tools-and-initiatives-in-the-Philippines-May2023.pdf)). These methods play on emotions, encouraging people to share content impulsively — often without verifying its accuracy. 

Fact-checking groups like VERA Files, [Tsek.ph](http://tsek.ph/), and FactsFirstPH work hard to expose false claims. However, their capacity is limited. Misinformation spreads rapidly — often faster than these organizations can respond — especially during critical events like elections or national crises. 

That’s why learning to fact-check independently is more important than ever. Waiting for someone else to verify information isn’t always enough — sometimes, the responsibility falls on us.

## Why We Can’t Rely Solely on Fact-Checkers

Misinformation is like wildfire — it spreads fast, often faster than fact-checkers can respond. 

During major events like elections or national crises, false claims flood social media, drowning timelines in misleading content. By the time fact-checkers step in to debunk these claims, the damage is often done. People have already seen it, believed it, and in some cases, shared it. 

But misinformation doesn’t just spread randomly — it’s often pushed by coordinated troll networks that operate like well-oiled machines. 

These networks aren’t just noisy — they’re strategic. Troll farms work to dominate conversations and silence those who challenge false claims. 

They use several tactics:

Mass Posting: Trolls flood comment sections and forums with identical or similar posts, making fake narratives appear popular or widely accepted.

Attacking Dissenters: Anyone who dares to challenge false information — even with credible sources — risks being swarmed with insults, harassment, or threats. It’s a tactic designed to intimidate people into silence.

Amplifying Confusion: Trolls deliberately mix real facts with false claims, blurring the line between truth and deception. This makes it harder for the average person to tell what’s real and what’s manipulated.

Even when fact-checkers step in, these troll campaigns often succeed in keeping false narratives louder and more visible than the truth.

## Not All False Information Is the Same

To make things even more complicated, false information comes in different forms — and each type requires a different approach to verification.

Misinformation is false information shared without malicious intent — like when a friend unknowingly reposts a fake story, believing it’s true.

Disinformation is deliberately created to deceive — fabricated quotes, manipulated images, or staged videos designed to mislead.

Malinformation is real information twisted to cause harm — such as leaking private conversations out of context to damage someone’s reputation.

Each type demands attention — and relying on fact-checkers alone isn’t enough to stop it.

## The Risk of Blind Trust in Fact-Checkers

Even credible sources can make mistakes. 

I used to admire The Philippine Daily Inquirer. For years, they played a vital role in upholding democracy — challenging those in power, giving voice to the marginalized, and delivering news that mattered. 

But things changed. Ownership shifted, and with it came a noticeable shift in tone and coverage. What used to be a beacon of independent journalism began to feel different, and many long-time readers, including myself, began to question its editorial direction. 

That’s why blind trust — even in once-trusted names — is risky. Bias can slip in quietly, and if you’re not paying attention, you might find yourself consuming a version of the truth that’s been carefully curated.

## Taking Ownership of the Truth

While fact-checking organizations provide valuable insights, the responsibility doesn’t end with them. 

Learning to assess information on your own — asking Who shared this? What’s the motive? — is the best way to avoid being misled. Because in today’s digital landscape, misinformation isn’t just someone else’s problem. It’s ours. 

And the truth? That’s something worth protecting.

## How to Fact-Check Independently Before Sharing Information

Fact-checking doesn’t require special tools or professional training — it starts with asking the right questions and taking a moment to pause.

1. Ask Key Questions Before Sharing

Whenever you come across content that feels urgent, shocking, or emotionally charged, ask yourself:

Who shared this? Investigate the profile or page that posted the content. Is it a credible source, or does it have a history of spreading misleading information? Anonymous accounts and newly created profiles are common tools for spreading fake news.

What’s the motive? Consider why this content is being shared. Does it aim to inform, persuade, or provoke outrage? Emotional manipulation is a common tactic in disinformation campaigns.

Who benefits from this information? Ask yourself: Who gains if people believe this? Identifying who stands to profit — whether financially, politically, or socially — can reveal underlying agendas.

2. Verify the Source

Not all websites are trustworthy. Check if the information comes from a legitimate news outlet, government agency, or established organization. Be cautious with viral screenshots — they’re easily edited. If there’s no official link to support the claim, treat it as questionable.

3. Trace the Information to Its Origin

False information is often recycled or repackaged. One common tactic in the Philippines — particularly among DDS (Diehard Duterte Supporters) and their coordinated networks — is to take old photos or videos and re-contextualize them to suit a current narrative. 

For example, if they want to inflate the number of supporters at a rally, they might find a random video of a massive crowd from a concert or a religious event and claim it’s a political gathering. 

However, those who know how to investigate such content — by tracing the video’s origin or analyzing the metadata — can easily debunk these claims.

To verify visuals like these:

Reverse Image Search Tools:
[Google Images](https://images.google.com/) — Upload an image or paste its URL to find related images and their sources.
[TinEye](https://tineye.com/) — A powerful reverse image search tool that reveals where an image appears online.
[Yandex](https://yandex.com/images/) — Often better at identifying images that originate from non-Western sources.

Video Verification Tool:
[InVID](https://www.invid-project.eu/) — A browser extension designed to verify videos by identifying their origin, checking for manipulation, and providing context.

4. Cross-Check with Multiple Sources

If credible outlets aren’t covering the story, that’s a red flag. Look for multiple trustworthy sources reporting the same information. Be especially cautious if the content claims to reveal “shocking truths” that mainstream media is supposedly hiding. Reliable stories are usually reported across various outlets — even those with differing political views.

5. Check the Date and Context

Old news often resurfaces to manipulate public opinion. Confirm when the event occurred. Outdated stories are sometimes reshared to create confusion or stir emotions. Pay close attention to headlines — manipulative titles are often exaggerated or distort the actual content of the story.

6. Spot Emotional Manipulation

Content designed to trigger strong emotions — especially anger, fear, or outrage — often bypasses logical thinking. Trolls and disinformation campaigns use emotional language to push people into reacting impulsively. If a post feels like it’s demanding an immediate emotional response, take a step back and question why.

7. Consult Trusted Fact-Checking Tools as a Reference (Optional)

While this guide encourages independent verification, tools like [Snopes](https://www.snopes.com/), [FactCheck.org](http://factcheck.org/), and [Break the Fake Movement](https://breakthefakemovement.com/) can still provide useful insights as a starting point.

Fact-checking independently isn’t complicated — it’s about slowing down, asking the right questions, and refusing to let emotions drive your decisions. DDS trolls — and similar groups that rely on deception — thrive when people accept false information at face value. By taking these steps, you deny them the power to mislead you.

## How to Handle Misinformation Once You’ve Identified It

Spotting fake news is one thing — deciding what to do next is another. Correcting false information isn’t just about proving someone wrong. Done poorly, it can backfire, turning a simple correction into an argument or making someone feel embarrassed. Worse, if you engage trolls the wrong way, you risk giving their lies even more attention. 

So how do you handle misinformation without adding fuel to the fire?

## Don’t Feed the Trolls

Trolls thrive on conflict. They’re not interested in truth — their goal is to provoke you. The louder and angrier you get, the more they win. 

I’ve seen this play out many times. Someone posts fake content, a well-meaning person tries to correct it, and before long, the comment section turns into a shouting match. Meanwhile, the original post gains even more visibility — and the troll walks away satisfied. 

Instead of engaging directly, focus on informing others quietly and strategically. The best way to beat trolls is to deny them the attention they crave.

## Correct Misinformation Without Embarrassment

It’s different when someone you know — like a friend or relative — unknowingly shares something false. 

Calling them out in public can make them defensive. No one likes feeling embarrassed, even if they’re wrong. 

A better approach? Send a private message. Something like: 

“Hey, I saw your post earlier. I looked into it, and it seems like it’s not accurate. Here’s what I found.” 

This way, they won’t feel attacked — and they’ll be more open to listening. 

If you share proof, stick to credible links instead of just saying “That’s fake!” People are more willing to reconsider their stance when you guide them gently instead of shaming them.

## Add Context, Not Conflict

When misinformation goes viral, sometimes the best move is to add context rather than confront the person who shared it. A calm, fact-based comment can make a huge difference. 

Instead of arguing, try something like: “This has been debunked — here’s a link that explains it better.” Keep it short and to the point. Trolls will try to bait you — don’t fall for it.

## Report the Lies

If you come across fake content designed to deceive, reporting it can help slow its spread. Most social media platforms have tools for this:

On Facebook, click the three dots on the post and select “Find support or report post.”

On Twitter (X), tap the three dots and choose “Report Tweet.”

On TikTok, hold the video, tap “Report,” then select “Misinformation.”

On YouTube, click the three dots below the video and select “Report.”

It may seem small, but these reports add up. When enough users report false content, platforms are more likely to take action.

## Teach, Don’t Argue

Some people simply don’t know how to fact-check — and that’s where you can make a real difference. Instead of pointing fingers, try teaching people how to spot fake content for themselves. Share useful tools like [Google Images](https://images.google.com/) for reverse searches or [InVID](https://www.invid-project.eu/) to check suspicious videos. Sometimes guiding people to tools they can explore on their own is far more effective than telling them what to believe.

## Know When to Walk Away

Some people won’t change their minds, no matter how much evidence you provide. If you find yourself stuck in an endless debate, it’s better to walk away. Not everyone is ready to accept the truth, and wasting your energy on a lost cause only leads to frustration. 

Correcting misinformation isn’t about winning arguments — it’s about protecting the truth without adding to the noise. 

If you approach people with patience and respect, they’re more likely to listen. And if you find yourself dealing with someone who refuses to listen, remember: sometimes the strongest thing you can do is simply stop engaging.

## Conclusion: The Power of Independent Fact-Checking

Misinformation isn’t just an accident — it’s often part of a larger strategy to manipulate what people believe and how they act. Coordinated troll networks, emotionally charged posts, and cleverly disguised lies are all designed to divide us and obscure the truth. 

That’s why independent fact-checking is so important. It doesn’t take special skills or fancy tools — just curiosity, common sense, and a moment of pause. 

Asking simple questions like “Who posted this?” or “What’s the motive?” can prevent you from spreading false information — and from becoming part of the problem. 

You don’t have to win arguments with trolls or change the minds of people who refuse to listen. Sometimes, the most important work is simply ensuring that you — and those close to you — are informed by facts, not fiction. 

Every time you choose facts over fake news, you’re helping build a better-informed society — one that values the truth over convenience. 

The truth may not always travel fast — but it’s worth protecting.

## You Think Duterte Is the Only Problem? Think Again.

The morning felt different today.
I sat with my coffee, letting the warmth seep into my hands, but my mind refused to settle. Lately, everything feels like noise. Every time I open social media or turn on the news, it’s the same cycle: Duterte vs. Marcos, accusations of betrayal, and a country caught in the crossfire.
It’s not that Duterte’s controversies aren’t serious — they are. But while everyone’s eyes are locked on the drama surrounding the former president and his family, I can’t help but feel that we’re missing something far more significant happening right under our noses.
Ferdinand “Bongbong” Marcos Jr. has quietly been steering the country into troubling waters — and he’s doing it while everyone is looking the other way. His leadership, marked by historical denialism ([Boell Foundation](https://th.boell.org/en/2022/09/23/martial-law-50)), corruption concerns ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bongbong_Marcos)), and ongoing human rights issues ([Benar News](https://www.benarnews.org/english/news/philippine/us-says-rights-abuses-continue-under-marcos-04232024125730.html)), carries risks that deserve attention.
The noise surrounding the Duterte family — Rodrigo Duterte’s ICC case, Sara Duterte’s feud with Marcos ([The Diplomat](https://thediplomat.com/2024/11/high-level-corruption-probe-leads-to-insults-and-death-threats-in-the-philippines/)). While this drama dominates the headlines, Marcos Jr.’s leadership slips by with little scrutiny.
And that’s what unsettles me the most.
We’re so busy fighting over Duterte that we risk overlooking an equally greater threat unfolding right now.

## The False Binary: Why Filipinos Are Stuck in a Divisive Loop

I was halfway through my second cup of coffee when I saw it — another heated exchange on Facebook.
One comment read, “If you hate Duterte, you must be a Marcos loyalist.”
followed by another saying, “If you criticize Marcos, you’re just a DDS troll.”
Conversations like this aren’t new. Politics in the Philippines has become a game of sides — you’re either with one dynasty or the other. There’s no middle ground, no space for nuanced critique.
Somewhere along the way, people stopped listening. Criticizing Duterte automatically makes you pro-Marcos in the eyes of some, and vice-versa. It’s a false binary that serves only those in power.
This mindset has done more than just divide us. It’s blinded many to what’s happening right in front of them.
Marcos Jr.’s leadership isn’t without its own set of problems. He has actively tried to reframe his family’s history, presenting the martial law era as a “golden age” despite evidence to the contrary ([Boell Foundation](https://th.boell.org/en/2022/09/23/martial-law-50)).
But that’s far from the truth. Martial law was a time of brutal repression, corruption, and economic collapse. To pretend otherwise isn’t just a difference of opinion — it’s an erasure of the victims’ suffering.
It’s a dangerous way of thinking.
The truth is, revising history has been happening quietly for years — not just under Marcos Jr., but through systematic efforts to change textbooks and public perception.
I’ve written before about how easily a revolution like EDSA 1986 could be erased if those in power control the narrative ([Morning Coffee Thoughts](https://www.ginoborlado.org/how-to-erase-a-revolution-if-i-were-in-power-how-would-i-make-filipinos-forget-edsa-1986)). Sadly, it feels like that warning is unfolding before our eyes.
When people are too focused on Duterte, it becomes easier to overlook how Marcos Jr. has dismissed legitimate concerns about his family’s past and current wealth ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bongbong_Marcos)).
If he were serious about accountability, he would have acknowledged the sins of his parents — the plunder and the human rights violations. Instead, he offers vague calls for “unity” that seem designed to silence dissent rather than address injustice.
And that’s what lingers in my mind.
It shouldn’t be this hard to just want better for the country without being accused of choosing sides.
If we can’t break free from this ‘us vs. them’ mentality, we risk losing sight of the bigger picture: that both dynasties have failed the Filipino people in their own ways.

## The Distraction Tactic: How Duterte's Drama Is Shielding Marcos Jr.

The news feels like a never-ending loop lately.
Sara Duterte resigning from Marcos Jr.’s cabinet. Accusations flying back and forth. Threats. Rodrigo Duterte’s latest tirade.
The noise keeps getting louder — arguments online, debates in coffee shops, endless political talk shows. It’s exhausting.
And in all that chaos, something doesn’t sit right.
While this political storm rages, Marcos Jr. keeps moving quietly in the background.
Amid the tension, Sara Duterte was subpoenaed for allegedly threatening to have Marcos, his wife, and the Speaker of the House killed ([The Diplomat](https://thediplomat.com/2024/11/high-level-corruption-probe-leads-to-insults-and-death-threats-in-the-philippines/)). Around the same time, Rodrigo Duterte’s ICC case intensified, fueling even more division.
While these stories dominate the headlines, Marcos Jr.’s troubling actions continue with little attention.
In 2024, a ₱276 million corruption case tied to his family’s ill-gotten wealth was dismissed — not because they were innocent, but because the original documents were missing ([Benar News](https://www.benarnews.org/english/news/philippine/philippines-anti-graft-court-dismisses-5m-ill-gotten-wealth-case-against-the-marcoses-10092024144834.html)). Whether that outcome was influenced by Marcos Jr. or simply the result of a flawed justice system, it represents a significant failure of accountability.
Even the human rights issues Marcos Jr. once promised to address persist under his watch. Reports of extrajudicial killings and harassment of activists remain a reality ([Benar News](https://www.benarnews.org/english/news/philippine/us-says-rights-abuses-continue-under-marcos-04232024125730.html)).
Maybe this constant Duterte drama isn’t intentional — but it’s hard to ignore how convenient it has become for the administration.
While Filipinos argue about one family, another quietly holds the reins of power with barely a whisper of protest.

## The ‘Lesser Evil’ Mindset: Why Filipinos Are Tolerating Marcos Jr.

A few weeks ago, I was talking to someone about politics. Somewhere in the conversation, they shrugged and said, “At least Marcos isn’t as loud as Duterte. He seems more professional.”
I understood what they meant. After years of hearing Duterte’s harsh words and watching his aggressive style, Marcos Jr.’s calm demeanor feels like a relief to some.
It’s not that they admire Marcos Jr. or believe he’s free of faults. It’s that his quieter approach makes it easier to look away from the problems that still exist.
That mindset — while understandable — carries risks.
Marcos Jr. has built his image around being the calm, diplomatic leader who speaks of unity instead of war. He uses sophisticated language and avoids the public outbursts that characterized the previous administration.
But a quieter tone doesn’t erase what’s happened — or what’s still happening.
Marcos Jr. has consistently refused to acknowledge the dark legacy of his father’s regime. He continues to benefit from the wealth accumulated during those years while avoiding direct responsibility ([Boell Foundation](https://th.boell.org/en/2022/09/23/martial-law-50)).
If he truly believed in accountability, he would have recognized the plunder, the killings, and the disappearances. He hasn’t.
And that’s what makes this “lesser evil” mindset so dangerous.
Choosing someone because they seem quieter, calmer, or less aggressive creates a false sense of safety. It allows people to feel that things are getting better, even when the underlying structures of corruption and injustice remain unchanged.
But that quiet doesn’t mean corruption has disappeared. It doesn’t mean human rights abuses have ended. In some ways, a leader who does these things quietly is harder to hold accountable than one who does them loudly ([Benar News](https://www.benarnews.org/english/news/philippine/us-says-rights-abuses-continue-under-marcos-04232024125730.html)).
I haven’t seen much coverage since then. Maybe things have improved. Or maybe, with less noise surrounding them, people have just stopped paying attention.
I wonder how much longer Marcos Jr. can keep moving quietly before people realize the damage has already been done.

## Filipino Fatigue: The Emotional Exhaustion That's Making People Indifferent

I used to wonder why some people stopped caring.
Not the ones who never paid attention — but the ones who once did. The ones who used to argue about the news, who showed up at rallies, who felt every scandal deeply.
Now, many of them just shrug.
It’s not that they’ve stopped noticing. It’s that they’re tired.
Years of broken promises, scandals, and corruption have worn people down. Every election, someone promises change, and every time, the same families end up in power.
That kind of disappointment builds up over time. After a while, it’s easier to just tune it all out.
But this exhaustion isn’t just about frustration — it’s about what people have been conditioned to accept.
Duterte’s presidency wasn’t just loud — it was brutal. Night after night, news of bodies on the streets, of families begging for justice, of a "war" that seemed to target the poorest among us.
Those images and stories played on repeat until death itself felt ordinary.
And when violence becomes background noise, people stop asking questions.
That’s what I notice now when I hear DDS supporters arguing. It’s not that they’re blind to what happened; it’s that they’ve normalized it as the price of “order.”
It’s as if the violence that once outraged people has been buried under layers of anger, loyalty, and exhaustion.
And now? Many Filipinos have reached a point where frustration has turned into quiet acceptance. Not because they agree with everything, but because they don't have the energy to fight anymore.
Somewhere along the way, we stopped counting the dead.
Somewhere along the way, we forgot how to grieve.
And I don’t know if we’ll ever find our way back from that.

## What Happens Next? The Risk of History Repeating Itself

The noise will die down eventually.
The headlines about Duterte’s ICC case will fade. The heated arguments about Sara Duterte and Marcos Jr.’s rift will be replaced by something else.
And when that happens, what’s left?
Moments like this — when people are tired, distracted, or numb — create the perfect space for history to be rewritten.
It doesn’t always start with grand speeches or bold declarations. Sometimes, it’s quieter — a textbook change here, a court dismissal there, a slow shift in how we talk about the past.
You don’t always notice it at first.
But suddenly, people start talking about martial law as if it was necessary — even heroic. The corruption that once defined a regime becomes a "misunderstanding," and the wealth that was stolen becomes "rightfully theirs."
The killings, the disappearances, the stories of families still grieving — they get pushed further into the margins until they’re forgotten altogether.
We’ve seen this before.
There was a time when people believed Ferdinand Marcos Sr. was untouchable — that no scandal, no corruption, and no amount of repression could ever bring him down.
But history shows that those moments of power don’t last forever.
Recently, Sara Duterte asked, 'Do we still have a country by 2028?' My initial thought was simple — yes, we will. But her question isn’t really about the survival of the Philippines. It’s about the survival of her family’s political future.
Sara Duterte’s ongoing feud with Marcos Jr. hasn’t just weakened her influence — it’s given Marcos Jr. the opportunity to isolate her.
The Dutertes may have once dominated headlines, but Marcos Jr. knows that as long as they remain the primary target of public anger, he can continue to consolidate his power without significant opposition.
Marcos Jr. doesn’t need to destroy Duterte’s legacy outright — he just needs to make sure her political influence is neutralized by 2028.
If that happens, the Dutertes’ influence will fade, and whatever power they once held will become a memory.
That’s how dynasties die — not always through scandal or exposure, but through silence and isolation.
The problem is, these moments of political erasure often come with a cost. When power struggles unfold at the highest levels, it’s rarely the politicians who suffer the most. It’s the people who are left behind in the chaos.
That’s what unsettles me now — this feeling that we’re slipping back into a cycle we’ve already lived through, but this time, we’re too tired to notice.
The signs are there — the quiet revision of history, the fatigue that’s turning into apathy, the gradual consolidation of power.
If that’s where we’re heading, I worry we won’t even notice it until it’s too late.
Because the real danger isn’t just in what leaders do.
It’s in what people stop believing they can change.

## Conclusion: The Real Threat Isn’t Just One Man

For many Filipinos, Duterte feels like the biggest threat the country has faced in recent years. His rhetoric, his policies, and the violence associated with his term have left deep scars.
But while people stay focused on him — debating whether he deserves punishment or protection — someone else is reaping the benefits of that distraction.
Marcos Jr. isn’t just another politician navigating the aftermath of Duterte’s presidency. He’s part of a legacy that has spent decades preparing for this moment.
The Marcos name has survived scandals, corruption cases, and decades of unanswered questions. And now, they are back in the seat of power, moving quietly, rewriting history, and waiting for the noise to settle.
You think Duterte is the only problem?
Think again.
Because the danger isn’t just about Duterte or Marcos Jr. — it’s about what happens when people stop paying attention. It’s about what happens when a country becomes so exhausted by the noise that it accepts silence as peace.
That’s how history is rewritten. That’s how corruption survives. That’s how silence becomes the greatest weapon of those in power.
We will still have a country by 2028.
But what kind of country it will be — one that remembers or one that forgets — depends on whether Filipinos can look past the noise before it’s too late.
